title: Dog Snorting When Excited: Is Reverse Sneezing an Emergency?
meta_desc: Learn why dogs snort when excited, what reverse sneezing is, and when to seek veterinary care for your pet's respiratory sounds.
content_html:
TL;DR: Reverse sneezing is a common, benign respiratory event in dogs that sounds like rhythmic honking and usually resolves on its own within seconds.

What causes my dog to make a sudden rhythmic snorting or honking sound?

If your dog makes a sudden, rhythmic snorting or honking sound when they get excited, they are likely experiencing what is known as a reverse sneeze. Technically called paroxysmal respiration, this occurs when the soft palate or throat becomes irritated, causing the dog to rapidly pull air in through their nose. It can look alarming, but it is a very common occurrence in many breeds, especially smaller dogs and those with flat faces.

When should I be concerned that my dog's snorting is a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for mild snorting during excitement is Low, and most cases do not require medical intervention.
  • Episodes typically last only a few seconds to a minute.
  • The condition is considered benign as long as your dog's tongue and gums remain pink.
  • Your dog should return to their normal self immediately after the episode ends.

How does a video or photo of these episodes help the veterinary triage team?

  • Capturing a video is essential because these episodes are often over by the time you arrive at a veterinary clinic.
  • A video allows a veterinarian to hear the specific sound and observe body language to confirm it is a reverse sneeze.
  • Visual recordings help rule out more serious issues like tracheal collapse, heart conditions, or a true cough.
  • While a photo shows resting posture, the movement and sound in a video provide the necessary context for an accurate assessment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Aspiration reflex, also known as reverse sneezing, is a short, paroxysmal episode of inspiratory effort to clear the nasopharynx of obstructing material and may be elicited by mild excitement. Dogs with acute rhinitis may exhibit sneezing to clear the upper airways of discharge or exudate. P caninum infection in dogs can cause sneezing, reverse sneezing, and rhinitis. Underlying neurologic disorders, especially in dogs, may rarely manifest with upper respiratory signs.

Chapter: Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1489)

faqs.question: What triggers a reverse sneezing episode?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include high excitement, pulling on a leash, irritants like perfumes or pollen, and eating or drinking too quickly.

faqs.question: How can I help my dog during a snorting fit?
faqs.answer: You can often stop the episode by gently massaging your dog's throat or briefly covering their nostrils to encourage them to swallow, which helps clear the irritation.

faqs.question: When does snorting become a concern?
faqs.answer: You should contact a vet if the snorting becomes frequent, if your dog is fainting, if there is bloody nasal discharge, or if the dog has difficulty breathing even when they are calm.
